content:
# Titus Chapter 2 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 222-223 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/TIT_002_page_001.jpg

1. But as for you, speak the things that are fitting for sound teaching:

2. that older men be sober-minded, dignified, self-controlled, sound in faith, in love, in endurance;

3. likewise older women, in deportment befitting holiness, not slanderers, not enslaved to much wine, teachers of what is good,

4. so that they may train the young women to be lovers of their husbands, lovers of their children,

5. self-controlled, pure, workers at home, good, being subject to their own husbands, so that the word of God may not be reviled.

6. Likewise, exhort the younger men to be self-controlled

7. in all things, showing yourself a pattern of good works; in your teaching showing integrity, dignity,

8. sound speech that is beyond reproach, so that the one who opposes may be put to shame, having nothing evil to say about us.

9. Bondservants are to be subject to their own masters in all things, to be well-pleasing, not contradicting,

10. not pilfering, but showing all good faithfulness, so that they may adorn the teaching of God our Savior in all things.

11. For the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation to all people,

12. training us, so that, having denied ungodliness and worldly desires, we might live self-controlled and righteously and godly in the present age,

13. awaiting the blessed hope and the appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior Jesus Christ,

14. who gave himself for us, that he might redeem us from all lawlessness and purify for himself a people for his own possession, zealous for good works.

15. Speak these things, and exhort and rebuke with all authority. Let no one despise you.

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

**Verse 2 — "sober-minded" (nhfalious / nephalious):** Sinaiticus reads ΝΗΦΑΛΙΟΥΣ, the standard form. Rendered "sober-minded" rather than merely "sober" to capture both the literal (temperate in drink) and extended (clear-headed) senses of the Greek.

**Verse 3 — "deportment befitting holiness" (katasthmati ieroprepeij / katastēmati hieroprepeis):** The compound ΙΕΡΟΠΡΕΠΕΙΣ ("befitting the sacred/holy") is rendered with the phrase "befitting holiness" to preserve the temple-sacred connotation of the iero- prefix, which "reverent" alone would flatten.

**Verse 5 — "workers at home" (oikourgous):** Sinaiticus reads ΟΙΚΟΥΡΓΟΥΣ ("workers at home") rather than the Byzantine variant ΟΙΚΟΥΡΟΥΣ ("keepers of the home"). The Sinaiticus form emphasizes active domestic labor rather than passive guardianship. Translated accordingly.

**Verse 7 — "integrity, dignity" (afqorian, semnothta / aphthonian, semnotēta):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΦΘΟΡΙΑΝ (incorruptibility/integrity) rather than the later variant ΑΔΙΑΦΘΟΡΙΑΝ. Both point to uncorrupted teaching. Rendered "integrity" to capture the sense of teaching free from corruption.

**Verse 8 — "about us" (peri hmwn / peri hēmōn):** Sinaiticus reads ΗΜΩΝ ("us") rather than the variant ΥΜΩΝ ("you"). This first-person reading includes Paul alongside Titus in the potential object of criticism.

**Verse 10 — "pilfering" (nosfizomenous / nosphizomenous):** ΝΟΣΦΙΖΟΜΕΝΟΥΣ specifically denotes secret misappropriation or embezzlement. "Pilfering" preserves the sense of petty, secretive theft rather than open robbery.

**Verse 11 — "bringing salvation to all people" (swthrioj pasin anqrwpoij / sōtērios pasin anthrōpois):** The adjective ΣΩΤΗΡΙΟΣ modifies the grace of God. The Sinaiticus text presents the grace as inherently salvific and universal in its manifestation, though "appeared" (ΕΠΕΦΑΝΗ) governs the clause. Rendered to preserve both the salvific character and universal scope.

**Verse 13 — "our great God and Savior Jesus Christ":** The Granville Sharp construction (single article TOU governing both ΘΕΟΥ and ΣΩΤΗΡΟΣ joined by ΚΑΙ with the proper noun following) strongly supports the reading that identifies Jesus Christ as both "great God" and "Savior." This is one of the most explicit affirmations of Christ's deity in the Pauline corpus.

**Verse 14 — "a people for his own possession" (laon periousion / laon periousion):** ΠΕΡΙΟΥΣΙΟΝ echoes the LXX designation of Israel as God's treasured/special possession (Exod 19:5; Deut 7:6; 14:2). The translation preserves this covenantal resonance.

**General note on Sinaiticus distinctives:** The text of Titus 2 in Sinaiticus is largely stable and agrees with the critical text (NA28/UBS5) in most readings. The primary variants noted above (ΟΙΚΟΥΡΓΟΥΣ in v. 5, ΑΦΘΟΡΙΑΝ in v. 7, ΗΜΩΝ in v. 8) are all well-attested early readings that the critical editions also favor.

--- LOGOS
